title: Organophosphate poisoning — decon, atropine, oxime, airway
briefing: You are running resus when EMS patches through: a farm worker found collapsed next to a pesticide sprayer, clothing soaked through. He is bradycardic with pinpoint pupils, drenched in sweat and secretions, gurgling with every breath. The crew reports HR in the 40s and an SpO2 of 82% despite high-flow oxygen. They are ninety seconds from your doors.

references.citation: Eddleston M, Buckley NA, Eyer P, Dawson AH. Management of acute organophosphorus pesticide poisoning. Lancet. 2008;371(9612):597-607.
references.note: Atropine titrated in doubling doses until chest secretions clear, with no fixed maximum; pralidoxime given early and slowly, alongside atropine.

references.citation: Eyer P. The role of oximes in the management of organophosphorus pesticide poisoning. Toxicol Rev. 2003;22(3):165-190.
references.note: Oximes reactivate inhibited acetylcholinesterase only before the phosphylated enzyme ages; delay reduces the chance of reactivation.
Succinylcholine is metabolized by plasma (butyryl)cholinesterase, which organophosphates inhibit, producing markedly prolonged paralysis; general cholinergic toxidrome management.

physician: Recognize severe cholinergic crisis and identify bronchorrhea and bronchospasm as the immediate airway threat driving hypoxia.
Apply an atropine strategy of doubling doses every 3-5 minutes titrated to clearing chest secretions, with no fixed ceiling dose.
Sequence definitive care correctly: pralidoxime given early and slowly alongside atropine, and rocuronium rather than succinylcholine for rapid sequence intubation.
nursing: Prepare and administer escalating atropine doses on a 3-5 minute cycle and recognize the titration endpoint: drying secretions and a chest that is clearing.
Set up pralidoxime as a slow intravenous infusion over 15-30 minutes rather than a rapid push, while atropine dosing continues.
Monitor continuous SpO2, heart rate, and secretion burden during titration, and report symptoms in exposed staff immediately.
ems: Recognize the cholinergic toxidrome in the field: pinpoint pupils, secretions from every orifice, wheezing, bradycardia, and sweating after pesticide contact.
Perform hot-zone decontamination — remove and bag contaminated clothing and wash the skin — before the patient enters a clean clinical space.
Protect the crew: don appropriate PPE and prevent secondary contamination of vehicles, equipment, and receiving staff.

cme.note: Draft content structured to CME/CE-style learning objectives; not accredited.

inbound.note: Severe cholinergic crisis — airway filling with secretions

decon_run.note: Hot-zone decon — suction and oxygen running in PPE

contaminated_resus.note: In resus, still soaked — staff working unprotected

atropine_working.note: Doubling atropine q3-5 min — chest starting to clear

atropine_working_contaminated.note: Patient improving — two exposed staff symptomatic next door

crashing.note: Bagging against a flooded airway — 2 mg was never enough

crashing_contaminated.note: Airway flooding — and two of your staff are now patients

drowning.note: Profound bradycardia, effort failing — secretions unopposed

definitive_care.note: Intubated with rocuronium — 2-PAM infusing, atropine continuing

oxime_delayed.note: Atropinized and tubed — but the inhibited enzyme is aging, untreated

prolonged_paralysis.note: Succinylcholine given — hours later, still not moving

door_choice.prompt: The crew is at the doors. The patient is soaked in pesticide, gurgling, SpO2 falling through the low 80s. What happens at the threshold?

options.label: Hot-zone decon first
options.detail: Strip, bag, wash outside the doors — suction and oxygen continue in PPE
options.log: Clothing stripped and bagged in the hot zone; skin decon running. Suction and high-flow O2 continue. The department stays clean.

options.label: Straight to resus — he's dying at the door
options.log: Patient rolled into resus in soaked clothing. Six minutes later an RN reports blurred vision and salivation; a tech follows. Two staff are now casualties and the bay is contaminated.

atropine_choice.prompt: Atropine 2 mg IV is in. Three minutes later the chest is still full, secretions pouring, SpO2 still falling. Next move?

options.label: Double the dose and repeat q3-5 min until secretions dry
options.detail: Titrate to a clearing chest — no fixed maximum
options.log: Atropine 4 mg... 8 mg... 16 mg. Suction demands falling, air entry returning.

options.label: Wait for the first 2 mg to take full effect
options.log: Waiting on 2 mg. The secretions are not waiting — SpO2 falling through the 70s.

atropine_choice_contaminated.prompt: Atropine 2 mg IV is in. Three minutes later the chest is still full, secretions pouring, SpO2 still falling. Next move?

options.label: Double the dose and repeat q3-5 min until secretions dry
options.detail: Titrate to a clearing chest — no fixed maximum
options.log: Atropine 4 mg... 8 mg... 16 mg. The chest is clearing. Next door, two exposed staff are being decontaminated and monitored.

options.label: Wait for the first 2 mg to take full effect
options.log: Waiting on 2 mg. The secretions are not waiting — SpO2 falling through the 70s.

atropine_rescue.prompt: HR 38 and falling, SpO2 in the 60s, suction cannot keep up. Last window.

options.label: Escalate now — doubling doses q3-5 min until the chest dries
options.log: Aggressive doubling started — late, but the chest begins to dry and the heart rate climbs.

options.label: Give the atropine another few minutes
options.detail: It should be working by now
options.log: The secretions never stopped. The bradycardia deepens.

atropine_rescue_contaminated.prompt: HR 38 and falling, SpO2 in the 60s, suction cannot keep up. Last window.

options.label: Escalate now — doubling doses q3-5 min until the chest dries
options.log: Aggressive doubling started — late, but the chest begins to dry and the heart rate climbs.

options.label: Give the atropine another few minutes
options.detail: It should be working by now
options.log: The secretions never stopped. The bradycardia deepens.

oxime_airway.prompt: Atropine is winning — secretions drying, HR 88. He remains obtunded and fatiguing, and needs a definitive airway. Set the next orders.

options.label: Pralidoxime (2-PAM) now: 1-2 g IV over 15-30 minutes, continue atropine — RSI with rocuronium
options.detail: Reactivate the enzyme before it ages; nondepolarizing paralytic
options.log: 2-PAM infusing slowly alongside continued atropine. Rocuronium RSI — first-pass tube, ventilator settings dialed in.

options.label: Hold the 2-PAM until he is fully atropinized — RSI with rocuronium
options.log: Rocuronium RSI succeeds — but the oxime sits in pharmacy while the phosphylated enzyme ages.

options.label: 2-PAM now — RSI with succinylcholine
options.detail: The paralytic you reach for every day
options.log: Succinylcholine given. The plasma cholinesterase that should clear it is poisoned — the paralysis will last hours.

survived.label: Survived — clean department, enzyme reactivated
survived.debrief: Decon at the doors kept your staff off the patient list. Atropine doubled every 3-5 minutes until the chest cleared — the endpoint is dry secretions, not a milligram count. Pralidoxime went in early and slowly while atropine continued, and rocuronium secured the airway without borrowing an enzyme the poison had already taken.
teaching: Atropine in organophosphate poisoning is titrated in doubling doses every 3-5 minutes until chest secretions clear, with no fixed maximum — under-dosing is the classic lethal error (Eddleston et al., Lancet 2008).

Give pralidoxime early and slowly (over 15-30 minutes, not as a rapid push), alongside atropine — oximes can only reactivate acetylcholinesterase before the phosphylated enzyme ages (Eddleston et al., Lancet 2008; Eyer, Toxicol Rev 2003).

Choose rocuronium for RSI: succinylcholine depends on plasma cholinesterase for metabolism, and organophosphates inhibit it, producing markedly prolonged paralysis (Goldfrank's Toxicologic Emergencies, 11th ed).

survived_contaminated.label: Patient survived — two staff became casualties
survived_contaminated.debrief: The pharmacology was right; the door decision was wrong. Rolling a soaked patient into resus converted an RN and a tech into patients and shut down a contaminated bay. Decontamination is not a delay of care — for this poisoning, it is care.
teaching: Hot-zone decontamination — clothing off and bagged, skin washed — happens before the patient crosses into a clean clinical space; suction, oxygen, and early atropine can all be delivered in PPE during decon.
Secondary contamination of staff is a real and preventable failure mode of pesticide incidents: one skipped shower can multiply your patient count and cost you a resus bay.
The antidote strategy was correct: atropine doubled every 3-5 minutes to clear the chest, with no ceiling dose (Eddleston et al., Lancet 2008).

survived_complicated.label: Survived — avoidable pharmacology misstep
survived_complicated.debrief: Atropine saved the airway, but the final orders cost the patient: either the oxime waited while the phosphylated enzyme aged beyond rescue, or succinylcholine met a plasma cholinesterase the poison had already inhibited and the paralysis stretched into hours. Both errors are avoided by one rule — reactivate early and slowly, and paralyze with rocuronium.
teaching: Oximes only work before the inhibited enzyme ages: give pralidoxime early, alongside atropine, rather than waiting for full atropinization — delay reduces the chance of reactivation (Eyer, Toxicol Rev 2003; Eddleston et al., Lancet 2008).
Infuse pralidoxime slowly — 1-2 g IV over 15-30 minutes, not a rapid push — while atropine titration continues (Eddleston et al., Lancet 2008).

Succinylcholine is metabolized by plasma (butyryl)cholinesterase, which organophosphates inhibit — expect markedly prolonged paralysis; use rocuronium instead (Goldfrank's Toxicologic Emergencies, 11th ed).

died.label: Death — atropine under-dosed, airway drowned
died.debrief: Two milligrams was a starting dose, not a treatment. While the team waited for it to work, bronchorrhea and bronchospasm flooded the airway, hypoxia deepened the bradycardia, and the patient arrested with the antidote sitting in the drawer.
teaching: Atropine is titrated to effect — doubling every 3-5 minutes until chest secretions clear — with no maximum dose; waiting on a fixed small dose is the classic lethal error (Eddleston et al., Lancet 2008).
The killer in cholinergic crisis is the wet airway: bronchorrhea and bronchospasm cause the hypoxia that drives the terminal bradycardia — suction and oxygen only buy time while the atropine climbs.
Severe poisoning declares itself early; escalation should be immediate and continuous, not a rescue attempted after the SpO2 is already in the 60s (Goldfrank's Toxicologic Emergencies, 11th ed).
